import Helm from "../partials/helm/_helm-definition.mdx"
import GitStorage from "../partials/helm/_storing-charts-git.mdx"

# About Helm installations with Replicated

This topic provides an introduction to Helm installations for applications distributed with Replicated.

## Overview

<Helm/>

Replicated strongly recommends that all applications are packaged using Helm because many enterprise users expect to be able to install an application with the Helm CLI. 

Existing releases in the Replicated Platform that already support installation with Replicated KOTS and Replicated Embedded Cluster (and that include one or more Helm charts) can also be installed with the Helm CLI; it is not necessary to create and manage separate releases or channels for each installation method.

For information about how to install with Helm, see:
* [Installing with Helm](/vendor/install-with-helm)
* [Installing and Updating with Helm in Air Gap Environments](helm-install-airgap)

The following diagram shows how Helm charts distributed with Replicated are installed with Helm in online (internet-connected) customer environments:

<img src="/images/helm-install-diagram.png" alt="diagram of a helm chart in a custom environment" width="700px"/> 

[View a larger version of this image](/images/helm-install-diagram.png)

As shown in the diagram above, when a release containing one or more Helm charts is promoted to a channel, the Replicated Vendor Portal automatically extracts any Helm charts included in the release. These charts are pushed as OCI objects to the Replicated registry. The Replicated registry is a private OCI registry hosted by Replicated at `registry.replicated.com`. For information about security for the Replicated registry, see [Replicated Registry Security](packaging-private-registry-security).

For example, if your application in the Vendor Portal is named My App and you promote a release containing a Helm chart with `name: my-chart` to a channel with the slug `beta`, then the Vendor Portal pushes the chart to the following location: `oci://registry.replicated.com/my-app/beta/my-chart`.

Customers can install your Helm chart by first logging in to the Replicated registry with their unique license ID. This step ensures that any customer who installs your chart from the registry has a valid, unexpired license. After the customer logs in to the Replicated registry, they can run `helm install` to install the chart from the registry.

When an authenticated user pulls a Helm chart from the Replicated registry (with `helm pull`, `helm install`, or `helm upgrade`, or from any OCI-compatible client), the Replicated registry injects customer-specific values into the `global.replicated` key of the chart's `values.yaml` file before serving the chart. For more information about the values schema, see [Helm global.replicated Values Schema](helm-install-values-schema).

## Brand the Replicated SDK with a Helm alias {#brand-sdk-alias}

When you include the Replicated SDK as a dependency of your application Helm chart, the SDK is installed as a subchart and produces Kubernetes resources (Deployment, Service, ServiceAccount, Role, and so on) with the name `replicated`. For some vendors, exposing the `replicated` name to end customers in the cluster is undesirable because it conflicts with the branding of the application.

You can use Helm's [`alias`](https://helm.sh/docs/topics/charts/#tags-and-condition-fields-in-dependencies) field on a chart dependency to rename the SDK subchart so that the Kubernetes resources it creates use a name of your choosing. This lets you ship the SDK under your own product or company name without forking the SDK Helm chart. The alias only changes the local name that Helm uses to reference the dependency: the chart is still pulled from `oci://registry.replicated.com/library/replicated`, and all of the telemetry, license enforcement, and SDK API behavior is unchanged.

### Step-by-step

To brand the Replicated SDK by using the `alias` field:

1. In your application Helm chart `Chart.yaml` file, add the `alias` field to the SDK dependency. The alias must be a valid Kubernetes name (lowercase alphanumeric characters and `-`).

   For example, to alias the SDK as `mycompany-sdk`:

   ```yaml
   # Chart.yaml
   dependencies:
   - name: replicated
     alias: mycompany-sdk
     repository: oci://registry.replicated.com/library
     version: 1.19.3
   ```

1. In your application Helm chart `values.yaml` file, configure SDK Helm values under a key that matches the alias rather than the default `replicated` key. Helm scopes a subchart's values under its alias when one is set.

   For example:

   ```yaml
   # values.yaml
   mycompany-sdk:
     minimalRBAC: true
     commonLabels:
       app.kubernetes.io/part-of: mycompany
   ```

   For a complete list of supported SDK values, see the SDK Helm chart [`values.yaml`](https://github.com/replicatedhq/replicated-sdk/blob/main/chart/values.yaml) file in GitHub. For more information about customizing the SDK, see [Customize the Replicated SDK](/vendor/replicated-sdk-customizing).

1. Update the `charts/` directory to pull the SDK chart:

   ```bash
   helm dependency update
   ```

1. Package your application Helm chart:

   ```bash
   helm package .
   ```

1. Add the chart archive to a new release, promote it to a channel, and install the release with Helm. For more information, see [Install the Replicated SDK](/vendor/replicated-sdk-installing) and [Installing with Helm](/vendor/install-with-helm).

### Example result in the cluster

The following example shows the result of installing an application Helm chart named `myapp` that includes the SDK as a dependency aliased to `mycompany-sdk`. After running `helm install myapp oci://registry.replicated.com/myapp/stable/myapp`, the SDK resources in the cluster are named after the alias instead of `replicated`:

```bash
$ kubectl get deploy --namespace myapp
NAME            READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
myapp           1/1     1            1           45s
mycompany-sdk   1/1     1            1           45s

$ kubectl get serviceaccount,role,rolebinding --namespace myapp
NAME                                  SECRETS   AGE
serviceaccount/mycompany-sdk          0         45s

NAME                                              CREATED AT
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/mycompany-sdk-role   2026-04-29T18:02:11Z

NAME                                                       ROLE                            AGE
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/mycompany-sdk-rolebinding   Role/mycompany-sdk-role   45s
```

End customers see the alias name on every SDK-managed resource, so the SDK presents itself as part of your product rather than as a separately-branded `replicated` component.

## Limitations

* Helm CLI installations do not provide access to any of the features of the Replicated installers (Embedded Cluster, KOTS, kURL), such as:
    * The Replicated Admin Console
    * Strict preflight checks that block installation
    * Backup and restore functionality powered by Velero
    * Required releases with the **Prevent this release from being skipped during upgrades** option

* <GitStorage/>

  For more information about the `global.replicated` values injected by the Replicated registry, see [Helm global.replicated Values Schema](helm-install-values-schema).
